nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Koalas are mostly herbivorous marsupials that primarily eat eucalyptus leaves
nodes.description: Koalas have a specialized diet, primarily consisting of eucalyptus leaves, which vary in nutritional quality and toxicity.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Koalas have a strong memory and can remember the location of their preferred eucalyptus trees

nodes.description: They primarily feed on leaves found on the tips of the branches, which are more nutritious and less toxic.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Koalas occasionally consume small quantities of other leaves, bark, and flowers
nodes.description: While eucalyptus leaves are their primary food source, koalas may eat other types of leaves during droughts or when preferred trees are scarce.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Koalas may eat leaves of acacia, melaleuca, and other eucalyptus trees in certain situations
nodes.description: They are very selective and will only consume leaves similar in nutritional content and toxicity to their preferred eucalyptus leaves.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Efforts are being made to protect koala habitat and the eucalyptus trees they depend on
nodes.description: Governments and conservation organizations are working to conserve koala habitat, slow habitat loss, and preserve corridors of vegetation.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Protected areas have been established in Australia to conserve koala habitats

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Initiatives are in place to research koala biology and behavior for their health and well-being

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Brewarrina Shire Council v Beckhaus Civil Pty Ltd & 1 Or [2005] NSWCA 248 is a case concerning a dispute over a contract for the construction of flood levees
nodes.description: Case summary: Brewarrina Shire Council v Beckhaus Civil Pty Ltd & 1 Or [2005] NSWCA 248 involves a dispute between a local council and a civil construction company regarding defective work, delay, and termination of the contract for flood levees and associated works.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Main issues in Brewarrina Shire Council v Beckhaus Civil Pty Ltd & 1 Or [2005] NSWCA 248 include contract termination validity and contractor's claim rights
nodes.description: Main issues: 1. Validity of termination of the contract by the council and contractor's right to claim for work done prior to termination. 2. Application of the Building and Construction Industry Security of Payment Act 1999 (NSW) regarding progress payments and work suspension. 3. Availability of liquidated damages for delay and defective work under the contract. 4. Appropriate costs orders in the case.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Termination of contract allows homeowners to claim damages for defective or incomplete work
nodes.description: Significance of termination: A homeowner may face difficulties in claiming damages for defective or incomplete work if the building contract has not ended. Only delayed completion damages can be awarded while the contract remains in effect. The contract must end by valid termination or acceptance of repudiatory conduct.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Repudiatory conduct occurs when one party breaches a fundamental term of the contract
nodes.description: Repudiatory conduct: Examples include refusing to complete necessary work, failing to meet deadlines, refusing to rectify defective work, and abandoning the project. This conduct allows for termination of the contract.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Termination of a building contract can be carried out by mutual agreement or unilateral action
nodes.description: Termination: The process must follow proper procedures, including giving written notice and complying with notice periods required by the contract.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Once a building contract is terminated, homeowners may claim damages for defective or incomplete work
nodes.description: Effect of termination: Termination ends the builder's right to carry out further work, potentially leading to a claim for breach of contract for any defective or incomplete work prior to termination.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Blessed Sydney Constructions PL v Vasudevan case considered the impact of Brewarrina principle on renewal power
nodes.description: Case background: In Blessed Sydney Constructions PL v Vasudevan, the Appeal Panel addressed a building dispute where the owner sought to terminate the contract and claim damages for breach, leading to a renewal application for a money order.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] The Appeal Panel held that renewal orders were not constrained by the Brewarrina principle
nodes.description: Impact of Brewarrina principle: The contract had ended, allowing the owner to terminate for breach and seek a money order on renewal application.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Types of orders available on a renewal application include rectification orders and money orders
nodes.description: Types of orders: The Appeal Panel determined that a money order was available on renewal under HBA Sch 4 para 8, including rectification orders and any other order the Tribunal could have made originally.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Homeowners need to understand the implications of electing to terminate and seeking damages
nodes.description: Electing to terminate: The owner's election to terminate the contract and seek a money order allowed the Tribunal to make orders unconstrained by the Brewarrina principle.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Social media can provide emotional support for people who are isolated
nodes.description: Social media can help provide social support, especially for people who have limited social connections offline. (Source: Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology, 2015)

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Social media can lead to decreased face-to-face interaction
nodes.description: Excessive social media use can lead to a decline in face-to-face interactions, potentially weakening relationships. (Source: Psychology of Popular Media Culture, 2017)

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Facebook depression can lead to feelings of jealousy and insecurity in romantic relationships
nodes.description: Facebook depression can create feelings of jealousy, insecurity, and mistrust in romantic relationships, making partners feel inadequate. (Source: Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking, 2015)

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] User believes social media companies have a responsibility to address negative effects
nodes.description: User questions whether social media companies should do more to address the negative effects of social media on relationships and mental health.

nodes.title: [2023-02-20] Social media companies can implement design changes to promote healthier interactions
nodes.description: Social media companies can make design changes such as algorithmic transparency, content moderation, and incentivizing positive behavior to promote healthier interactions.
